message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The recent coronavirus pandemic follows in its early stages an almost exponential expansion, with the number of cases as a function of time reasonably well fit by
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) ∝
                  <jats:italic>e</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sup>
                    <jats:italic>αt</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sup>
                  , in many countries. We analyze the rate
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  in different countries, choosing as a starting point in each country the first day with 30 cases and fitting for the following 12 days, capturing thus the early exponential growth in a rather homogeneous way. We look for a link between the rate
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  and the average temperature
                  <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                  of each country, in the month of the epidemic growth. We analyze a
                  <jats:italic>base</jats:italic>
                  set of 42 countries, which developed the epidemic at an earlier stage, an
                  <jats:italic>intermediate</jats:italic>
                  set of 88 countries and an
                  <jats:italic>extended</jats:italic>
                  set of 125 countries, which developed the epidemic more recently. Fitting with a linear behavior
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                  ), we find increasing evidence in the three datasets for a decreasing growth rate as a function of
                  <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                  , at 99.66%C.L., 99.86%C.L. and 99.99995% C.L. (
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  -value 5 10
                  <jats:sup>
                    <jats:italic>−</jats:italic>
                    7
                  </jats:sup>
                  , or 5
                  <jats:italic>σ</jats:italic>
                  detection) in the
                  <jats:italic>base, intermediate</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>extended</jats:italic>
                  dataset, respectively. The doubling time is expected to increase by 40% 50%, going from 5° C to 25° C. In the
                  <jats:italic>base</jats:italic>
                  set, going beyond a linear model, a peak at about (7.7 ± 3.6)°
                  <jats:italic>C</jats:italic>
                  seems to be present in the data, but such evidence disappears for the larger datasets. Moreover we have analyzed the possible existence of a bias: poor countries, typically
                  <jats:italic>−</jats:italic>
                  located in warm regions, might have less intense testing. By excluding countries below a given GDP per capita from the dataset, we find that this affects our conclusions only slightly and only for the
                  <jats:italic>extended</jats:italic>
                  dataset. The significance always remains high, with a
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  -value of about 10
                  <jats:sup>
                    <jats:italic>−</jats:italic>
                    3
                  </jats:sup>
                  10
                  <jats:sup>
                    <jats:italic>−</jats:italic>
                    4
                  </jats:sup>
                  or less. Our findings give hope that, for northern hemisphere countries, the growth rate should significantly decrease as a result of both warmer weather and lockdown policies. In general the propagation should be hopefully stopped by strong lockdown, testing and tracking policies, before the arrival of the next cold season.
                </jats:p>
title: Temperature dependence of COVID-19 transmission

reference.unstructured: K. H. Chan , J. S. Malik Peiris , et al. The Effects of Temperature and Relative Humidity on the Viability of the SARS Coronavirus. Advances in Virology., Vol. 2011(Article ID 734690), 2011.
Mao Wang , Aili Jiang et al. Temperature significant change COVID-19 Transmission in 429 cities. 2020. https://www.medrxiv.org/content/early/2020/02/25/2020.02.22.20025791.
Miguel B. Araujo and Babak Naimi . Spread of SARS-CoV-2 Coronavirus likely to be constrained by climate. https://www.medrxiv.org/content/early/2020/03/16/2020.03.12.20034728. 2020.
Qasim Bukhari and Yusuf Jameel . Will Coronavirus Pandemic Diminish by Summer? ; available at ssrn: https://ssrn.com/abstract=3556998. 2020.

Mohammad M. Sajadi , Parham Habibzadeh , Augustin Vintzileos , Shervin Shokouhi , Fernando Miralles-Wilhelm , and Anthony Amoroso . Temperature, Humidity and Latitude Analysis to Predict Potential Spread and Seasonality for COVID-19 ; available at ssrn: https://ssrn.com/abstract=3550308 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3550308.

Wei Luo , Maimuna S Majumder , Dianbo Liu , Canelle Poirier , Kenneth D Mandl , Marc Lipsitch , and Mauricio Santillana . The role of absolute humidity on transmission rates of the COVID-19 outbreak. https://www.medrxiv.org/content/early/2020/02/17/2020.02.12.20022467. 2020.

In practice we choose, as the first day, the one in which the number of cases Ni is closest to 30. In some countries, such a number Ni is repeated for several days; in such cases we choose the last of such days as the starting point. For the particular case of China, we started from January 16th, with 59 cases, since the number before that day was essentially frozen.

The only two exceptions to this procedure are: Japan and U.S.A.. For Japan we have subdivided into three regions: Hokkaido, Okinawa and the rest of the country, using respectively the temperatures of Sapporo, Naha and Tokio. For the U.S.A. we used the national average of about 5.3 degrees from https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/sotc/national/201903. zFor Ecuador, we used the average T = 27.5°C of Guayaquil, the main site for the disease. For Brunei we used T = 27 °C and for Morocco we used the average Temperature for Casablanca, T = 14.7°C, from https://en.climate-data.org. For Jordan we used T = 17°C, from https://www.weather-atlas.com/en/jordan/amman-weather-march.

For China, South Korea, Singapore, Iran, Taiwan and Japan we considered an interpolating function of the temperature for the months of January, February and March and we took an average of such function in the relevant 12 days of the epidemic.
Only countries with at least 300.000 inhabitants have been considered in this dataset. SST
Here R 2 is defined as , where SSR is the residual sum of squares and SST is the sum of the squared differences between the α values and their mean value.
We used here data from https://ourworldindata.org/ on real GDP per capita, for the year 2017.
